Title: Fruits Produce Branched-Chain Esters Primarily from Newly Synthesized Precursors
Journal: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Common entry step for valine, leucine, and isoleucine precursor biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Branched-chain α-ketoacid pathway intermediates leading to valine, leucine, and isoleucine
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the common initial condensation step in branched-chain amino acid biosynthesis, producing precursors for branched-chain α-ketoacids and amino acids
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step/common enzyme; direct enzymatic target of inhibitor-based pathway interrogation
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Malus × domestica; Musa spp.; Chaenomeles × superba
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Gala; Empire; Jonagold; Red Delicious; Valery; Dr. Banks Pink
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Malus × domestica; Musa spp.; Chaenomeles × superba
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Gala; Empire; Jonagold; Red Delicious; Valery; Dr. Banks Pink
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Apple peel; banana pulp; likely flowering quince peel
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Chemical inhibition of AHAS strongly reduced branched-chain ester production and the corresponding branched-chain amino acid/α-ketoacid pools, supporting de novo precursor synthesis as the primary source of these esters
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Herbicide inhibitor treatments with sulfonylureas/imazamox; volatile profiling by SPME-GC-MS; amino acid/precursor profiling by UPLC-MS/MS and derivatization GC-MS; precursor feeding complementation with amino acids and α-ketoacids
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fruit ripening stage; postharvest storage state; exogenous ethylene treatment in banana; AHAS inhibitor treatments (rimsulfuron, halosulfuron-methyl, imazamox)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper uses AHAS as a functional enzymatic target rather than identifying a specific cloned gene locus; inhibitor efficacy may plateau around ~85%, so residual ester production may reflect incomplete inhibition. In apple, valine was unaffected and leucine increased despite strong isoleucine decrease, indicating branch-specific pathway programming during ripening. In banana, sec-branched volatiles were unaffected, indicating an AHAS-independent origin.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating AHAS-controlled flux or branch-specific upstream pathways could modulate branched-chain ester aroma, but AHAS is a central BCAA biosynthetic enzyme and broad suppression would likely be deleterious; better engineering targets may lie in branch-specific upstream modules
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Inhibiting AHAS reduced anteiso- and iso-branched-chain esters by ≥90% across apple, banana, and flowering quince tissues. 2. The corresponding branched-chain amino acid or precursor pools were depleted in parallel with volatile loss. 3. Feeding downstream precursors restored ester formation, showing that inhibitor-treated tissues retained biosynthetic capacity and that ester loss was due to precursor scarcity.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptomic datasets for AHAS catalytic subunit expression in ripening apple and banana were discussed, but accession numbers were not provided in the text supplied
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Catalytic subunit transcript reported as constitutively expressed during fruit development and increased during ripening by ~1.4-fold in apple and ~2.7-fold in banana
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: AHAS is essential for branched-chain amino acid biosynthesis; broad inhibition arrests growth and can cause severe physiological defects/plant death

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Citramalate-mediated isoleucine/anteiso-branched-chain precursor biosynthesis linked to branched-chain ester formation
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Citramalate, leading to α-ketobutyrate and then isoleucine-related precursors
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses acetyl-CoA and pyruvate to form citramalate, providing a noncanonical route to α-ketobutyrate for isoleucine-related volatile precursor biosynthesis in apple
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Appears to be a critical branch-specific control point for apple anteiso-branched-chain ester precursor supply
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides the α-ketobutyrate precursor supply required for isoleucine and anteiso-branched-chain ester accumulation in ripening apple fruit
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In this paper, inference from inhibitor experiments, stable-isotope acetate feeding, and prior characterization of cultivars lacking an active allele; not directly manipulated here
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fruit ripening stage; AHAS inhibitor treatment used to infer upstream carbon shunting; labeled acetate feeding
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This gene is discussed as a decisive upstream pathway component in apple, but it is not directly experimentally targeted in this paper’s Results section. AHAS inhibition likely shunts carbon toward straight-chain elongation via citramalate synthase, consistent with increased pentyl acetate/methyl pentanoate labeling.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for apple aroma engineering to enhance anteiso-branched-chain ester formation while bypassing canonical allosteric regulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Citramalate synthase is discussed as the apple-specific upstream source of α-ketobutyrate for anteiso-branched-chain metabolism. 2. Prior evidence cited indicates that cultivars lacking active citramalate synthase do not accumulate much isoleucine or anteiso-branched-chain esters. 3. The current inhibitor results are consistent with citramalate synthase being critical for precursor supply in ripening apple peel.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptomic data discussed for ripening apple peel expression increase (104.7-fold), accession not provided here
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Reported to increase 104.7-fold in ripening apple peel
